coredata.dc:description: Bacterial infections have emerged as an escalating threat to public health. Particularly, bacterial resistance to common antibiotics tremendously compels researchers to develop novel anti-bacterial agents. As a multifunctional carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), have been widely explored to fight against bacterial infections. In this study, environmentally friendly carbohydrazide (CDH)-based CDs (CDH–CDs) were obtained via a facile one-step hydrothermal method, which demonstrated superior anti-bacterial activity, while effectively eradicating biofilms. Compared with two typical antibiotics, rifampicin and kanamycin, no obvious resistance to CDH–CDs was developed for two kinds of drug-resistant bacteria. Research on mechanisms indicated that CDH–CDs killed the bacteria through the damage of bacterial cell walls and the impact on gene expression, then promoting the generation of endogenous reactive oxygen species (ROS). In vivo experiments further demonstrated the good anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs. This work presents a facile strategy to combat bacterial infection, while also uncovering new insights into the potential applications of CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of CDH–CDs. (A) One-step hydrothermal synthesis of CDH–CDs. (B) TEM image of CDH–CDs (inset: high-resolution TEM image). (C) Histogram of particle size distribution in B, analyzed by ImageJ. (D) PXRD pattern of CDH–CDs. (E) FT-IR spectra of CDH–CDs and the precursor CDH. XPS total curve (F), C1s (G), N1s (H), and O1s (I) high-resolution XPS curves of CDH–CDs.

In vitro anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs. Inhibition rate of CDH–CDs or CDH at different concentrations against (A) S. aureus, (B) E. coil, (C) MRSA, and (D) ESBL-E. Results are presented as mean ± S.D.; n = 3; n.s. not significant, p ≥ 0.05; *p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001; analyzed by Student’s t-test, CDH–CDs versus CDH-treated group. The photographic images (G) and the corresponding bar diagram of the ZOI developed during disk diffusion assays for CDH (indicated by the blue circle) and CDH–CDs (indicated by the black circle) against MRSA (E) and ESBL-E (F), respectively. Results are presented as mean ± S.D.; n = 3; ***p < 0.001; analyzed by Student’s t-test, CDH–CDs versus CDH-treated group.

In vitro anti-bacterial efficacy and bacterial resistance of CDH–CDs. The growth inhibition curves of CDH or CDH–CDs at different concentrations against MRSA (A) and ESBL-E (B). Results are presented as mean ± S.D.; n = 5; n.s. not significant, p ≥ 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001; analyzed by Student’s t-test, control versus other groups at the time point of 24 h. (C) Overlay images of live/dead staining of MRSA or ESBL-E incubated with or without CDH or CDH–CDs. Green channel, DMAO, live and dead bacteria, λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm. Red channel, PI, dead bacteria, λex = 552 nm, λem = 560–620 nm. (D) SEM images of MRSA or ESBL-E incubated with or without CDH or CDH–CDs. The surface shrinkage and fusion with holes were indicated with blue arrows. Resistance development of MRSA (E) and ESBL-E (F) to CDH–CDs, rifampicin, and kanamycin, respectively.

In vitro biofilm removal activity of CDH–CDs. Crystal violet staining photographs and removal rate of MRSA (A and B) or ESBL-E (C and D) biofilms after incubation with CDH or CDH–CDs at different concentrations. Results are presented as mean ± S.D., n = 5; ***p < 0.001; analyzed by Student’s t-test, CDH–CDs versus CDH-treated group. (E) Overlay images of live/dead staining of MRSA or ESBL-E biofilms after incubation with or without CDH or CDH–CDs. Green channel, DMAO, live and dead bacteria, λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm. Red channel, PI, dead bacteria, λex = 552 nm, λem = 560–620 nm.

Anti-bacterial mechanism of CDH–CDs. (A) ROS generation of MRSA incubating with sterile water (control), CDH–CDs, CDH–CDs and •OH scavenger IPA, CDH–CDs and 1O2 scavenger His, or CDH–CDs and O2 •- scavenger PBQ using DCFH-DA as the probe. The content of the ROS was proportional to the fluorescence intensity of DCF. Excitation: 488 nm, slit width 10 nm. (B) Fluorescence spectrum of MRSA incubated with TA, CDH–CDs, or CDH–CDs and TA. The increase in fluorescence intensity at 410 nm of TA indicated the generation of •OH. Excitation: 305 nm, slit width 10 nm. (C) 1O2 generation of MRSA incubated with CDH–CDs at different concentrations using DPBF as the probe. The content of the 1O2 is inversely proportional to the absorbance of DPBF. (D) O2 •- generation of MRSA incubated with CDH–CDs at different concentrations using cytochrome c as the probe. The absorbance at 550 nm (OD550) was proportional to the content of the O2 •-. Results are presented as mean ± S.D., n = 3, ***p < 0.001, analyzed by Student’s t-test, 0 μg/mL CDH–CDs-treated group versus other groups. (E) Relative expression levels of mRNA in MRSA incubated with CDH–CDs. Results are presented as mean ± S.D., n = 3. (F) Proposed anti-bacterial mechanism of CDH–CDs.

In vivo anti-bacterial activity of CDH–CDs. (A) Schematic illustration of the treatment protocol for MRSA-infected mice. (B) Photographs of wounds of MRSA-infected mice after different treatments. (C) Relative wound area of MRSA-infected mice after different treatments. Results are presented as mean ± S.D., n = 5, ***p < 0.001, analyzed by Student’s t-test, CDH-treated group versus other groups. (D) Schematic diagram of the wound in B. (E) H&E and Masson’s trichrome staining of the infected wounds at the end of the treatments. Scale bar, 200 μm.
H&E staining of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of healthy mice or MRSA-infected mice after different treatments. Scale bar, 200 μm.
The MIC (μg/mL) of CDs synthesized from different precursors. All CDs were synthesized under the same conditions.

head.ce:title: Simple easy-to-get carbohydrazide-based carbon dots with superior anti-bacterial activity to overcome bacterial resistance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial infections have emerged as an escalating threat to public health. Particularly, bacterial resistance to common antibiotics tremendously compels researchers to develop novel anti-bacterial agents. As a multifunctional carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs), have been widely explored to fight against bacterial infections. In this study, environmentally friendly carbohydrazide (CDH)-based CDs (CDH–CDs) were obtained via a facile one-step hydrothermal method, which demonstrated superior anti-bacterial activity, while effectively eradicating biofilms. Compared with two typical antibiotics, rifampicin and kanamycin, no obvious resistance to CDH–CDs was developed for two kinds of drug-resistant bacteria. Research on mechanisms indicated that CDH–CDs killed the bacteria through the damage of bacterial cell walls and the impact on gene expression, then promoting the generation of endogenous reactive oxygen species (ROS). In vivo experiments further demonstrated the good anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs. This work presents a facile strategy to combat bacterial infection, while also uncovering new insights into the potential applications of CDs.

Schematic illustrations of carbohydrazide-based CDs (CDH–CDs) to generate endogenous ROS and interfere with gene expression to efficiently kill bacteria for biofilm eradication and wound healing.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pathogenic bacterial infections represent a serious threat to worldwide public health, manifesting clinically as hyperpyrexia, suppurative inflammation, diarrheal disease, and other symptoms [1]. Without timely intervention, they can lead to organ failure or even death. Over the past few decades, bacterial infections have been effectively managed through antibiotic treatment, achieving significant clinical success. However, the abuse of antibiotics might bring about the emergence and rapid dissemination of bacterial resistance [2,3], which significantly prolongs hospital stays and increases fatality rates. Projections indicate that antimicrobial resistance (AMR) will directly cause over 39 million deaths worldwide by 2050 [4]. Moreover, residues from antibiotic-resistant pathogens can also enter the human body through environmental pathways (e.g., water, soil, and air), posing long-term health risks [5]. Particularly, with the emergence of biofilms, bacteria aggregate in extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) secreted by themselves, which provides bacteria with both environmental protection and AMR by enhancing tolerance to extreme conditions, and creating a physical barrier that limits drug diffusion [6]. Within the human body, biofilms colonize the surfaces of various organs and tissues, contributing to numerous infections including ocular, urinary tract, respiratory, and oral diseases. Additionally, pathogenic biofilms readily form on medical devices and implants, such as urinary catheters, artificial organs, orthopedic implants, and dental materials, often serving as persistent sources of infection [7]. Current research indicates that diverse bacterial and chronic infections are concerned with bacterial biofilm, which significantly enhances treatment resistance and clinical management challenges [8,9]. Consequently, developing therapeutic strategies that simultaneously exhibit potent antibacterial activity [10], inhibit biofilm formation [11], and circumvent bacterial resistance represents a critical unmet need in infectious disease management [12].

Conventional antibiotics effectively eliminate common bacteria by targeting conserved bacterial pathways, such as cell wall synthesis [13]. However, bacteria can evade eradication through various mechanisms, including the secretion of inactivating enzymes such as β-lactamase. Over time, these adaptations lead to the development of drug resistance, significantly diminishing the anti-bacterial efficacy of antibiotics [14]. Nevertheless, the development of alternative therapeutic methods cannot keep up with the evolution of bacteria. Confronting the critical challenges in current clinical anti-bacterial treatments, effective plans should be made to regulate the use of antibiotics to slow down the resistance development of bacteria and maintain the effectiveness of key medically available antibiotics [15,16]. Many countries have promulgated laws and regulations on the rational use of antibiotics. Besides, alternative antibacterial approaches, such as phage therapy [17], antimicrobial peptides [18], phototherapy [19–22], and immunotherapy [23], have been developed. However, these strategies still face significant limitations. For example, the clinical application of antimicrobial peptides remains significantly constrained by high production costs, low bioavailability, susceptibility to proteolytic degradation, and potential systemic toxicity [24].

With the development of nanotechnology [25,26], nanomaterials with antibacterial activity have been exploited widely [27,28]. These materials demonstrate unique functions due to their nanoscale dimensions and high surface-area-to-volume ratio, making them particularly promising for antibacterial applications [29]. Hitherto, diverse anti-bacterial nanoagents have been reported, including metal-based nanoparticles [30], graphene oxides [31], dendrimers [32], organic nanoparticles [33], etc. Wherein, carbon dots (CDs), a multifunctional carbon-based nanomaterial with a diameter below 10 nm, have demonstrated enormous potential in bioimaging [34], drug delivery [35], biosensing [36], tumor therapy [37], and anti-bacterial [38]. CDs typically feature a crystalline graphite-like core surrounded by an amorphous shell containing abundant surface functional groups, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide, and carbonyl moieties [39]. This distinctive architecture confers several exceptional properties on CDs: high aqueous solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and facile bioconjugation capability [40]. Notably, elemental doping represents a powerful strategy to engineer the properties of CDs. Studies have revealed that nitrogen incorporation enables precise control over nanostructure architecture, defect states, and optoelectronic characteristics [41–43]. Hydrazide derivatives, ordinary‌ raw materials for organic synthesis containing carbon and nitrogen elements, are desired precursors for the preparation of one-precursor-derived N-doping CDs [44,45]. However, as far as we know, there is a paucity of reports focusing on the anti-bacterial and anti-biofilm properties of CDs derived from this type of precursor.

Herein, in this work, a facile hydrothermal synthesis method was adopted to prepare novel N-doping CDs using diversified hydrazide derivatives as the precursor, and CDs originating from the cheap, safe, and environmentally friendly carbohydrazide (CDH–CDs) stood out for their excellent anti-bacterial performances. CDH–CDs showed the best anti-bacterial activity over other hydrazide derivative-based CDs, and had efficient anti-biofilm performance, and low capacity to induce bacterial resistance. Furthermore, CDH–CDs could damage and inhibit the repair of the bacterial cell walls, inducing endogenous reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to eliminate bacteria.

Hydrazide derivative-based CDs were synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal method using ten hydrazide derivatives as the precursor, respectively. Briefly, 120 mg carbohydrazide (CDH) or other hydrazide derivatives was dissolved in 12 mL double distilled water (ddwater). After 30 min ultrasonic treatment, the mixture was kept in an oven at the temperature of 180 °C with a reaction time of 12 h in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave reaction vessel and then cooled to room temperature [46–48]. The obtained mixture was dried through lyophilization, and CDs were dispersed in ddwater at 10 mg/mL for further use.

Zeta potentials were recorded by a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured through an N4S UV–VIS Spectrophotometer (INESA Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd, China). Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis was executed on the D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany). The JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) was utilized to obtain transmission electron microscope (TEM) images. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were mensurated with a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectroscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was done by ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, US).

The bacterial strains were acquired from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC), covering Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 35,218), extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-producing Enterobacteriaceae (ESBL-E, ATCC 25,922), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 6538), and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA, ATCC 43,300). The bacterial suspension was placed on sterilized tryptic soy broth (TSB) agar plates and cultured at 37 °C for 18 h. Later, a bacterial colony was singled out from the plate and cultured in liquid TSB overnight to obtain bacteria grown in the logarithmic phase for further use.

Bacteria with the inoculum size of 1 × 105 colony forming units (CFU)/mL were seeded into a 96-well plate (100 μL/well). 100 μL hydrazide derivative-based CDs were then mixed with the bacteria in the well with different concentrations. Bacteria incubated with CDs at the concentration of 0 μg/mL were used as the negative control. After the bacteria suspension was incubated at 37 °C with an incubation time of 18 h, the absorbance at 600 nm (OD600) in each well was detected using a microplate reader (DNM-9602, China). The trials were performed five times. The inhibition rate at each concentration was calculated using the formula (1). The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) was the minimum concentration showing an inhibition rate exceeding 90 %.

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was spread on the TSB or MacConkey (MAC) agar plates, respectively. Disk diffusion assays were conducted by placing sterilized Oxford cups on the plates, which were then filled with CDH or CDH–CDs (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, or 4.0 mg/mL, 100 μL). The agar plates were cultured at 37 °C for 18 h. The trials were performed three times.

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was inoculated in TSB with CDH or CDH–CDs at different concentrations (0, 12.5, 25.0, 50.0 μg/mL) at 37 °C. OD600 was measured every two hours till 24 h to monitor the bacterial growth inhibition. The trials were performed five times.

MRSA or ESBL-E growing in the log phase was collected by centrifugation and resuspended in 0.85 % NaCl solution. The bacteria (1 × 108 CFU/mL) were further incubated with or without 800 μg/mL CDH or CDH–CDs at 37 °C for 1 h. Then, live or dead bacteria were stained using a Bacterial Viability Assay Kit (C2030S, Beyotime) as per the manufacturer’s instructions and imaged by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). Green channel, N, N-dimethylaniline N-oxide (DMAO), live and dead bacteria, λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm. Red channel, propidium iodide (PI), dead bacteria, λex = 552 nm, λem = 560–620 nm.

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 109 CFU/mL) was incubated with or without 800 μg/mL CDH or CDH–CDs at 37 °C for 4 h, then collected by centrifugal separation. The collected bacteria were fixed and dehydrated. After being dried in a freeze dryer and treated with spray-gold, the samples were imaged with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6390LV, JEOL, Japan).

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was incubated with CDH–CDs, rifampicin, or kanamycin at different concentrations at 37 °C for 24 h to measure the MIC of each agent. Thereafter, bacteria in the 0.5-fold MIC suspension were added to fresh media at a density of 1 × 105 CFU/mL and further treated with CDH–CDs, rifampicin, or kanamycin at different concentrations. This was repeated for 14 passages.

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was inoculated in TSB with CDH or CDH–CDs at different concentrations (0, 25.0, 50.0, 100, 200, 400, 800 μg/mL), and cultivated at 28 °C for 72 h without agitation. Bacteria incubated without CDH or CDH–CDs were used as the negative control. After being rinsed 5 times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) to remove planktonic bacteria, the as-formed biofilms were fixed, washed, and dried. After that, the fixed biofilms were stained with 1 wt% crystal violet for 5 min, which were further rinsed with PBS 3 times. Finally, the stained biofilms were dissolved with 33.3 % acetic acid (200 μL/well), photographed by a smartphone, and measured absorbance at 570 nm (OD570) using a microplate reader. The trials were performed five times. The biofilm inhibition rate at each concentration was calculated using the formula (2).

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was incubated in TSB at 28 °C for 48 h without agitation to promote the formation of biofilms. After being rinsed 3 times with PBS, the biofilms were incubated with CDH or CDH–CDs at different concentrations (0, 25.0, 50.0, 100, 200, 400, or 800 μg/mL) for 24 h, and then fixed, stained, and dissolved before measurement of OD570. Biofilms incubated without CDH or CDH–CDs were used as the negative control. The trials were performed five times. The biofilm removal rate at each concentration was determined using the formula (3). The minimum concentration of antibacterial agent that can kill all the bacteria in the biofilm is defined as the minimum biofilm eradication concentration (MBEC) [49].

MRSA or ESBL-E (1 × 105 CFU/mL) was incubated in TSB containing round coverslip at 28 °C for 48 h to form biofilms, which were then treated with or without CDH or CDH–CDs at 50.0 μg/mL for 24 h, and then stained with DMAO and PI. The round coverslips were imaged by CLSM to estimate the biofilm removal efficiency of CDH–CDs.

Bacteria (1 × 108 CFU/mL) were incubated with 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescent diacetate (DCFH-DA) for 20 min, and further incubated with 200 μg/mL CDH–CDs, 200 μg/mL CDH–CDs and 10 mM hydroxyl radical (•OH) scavenger isopropanol (IPA), 200 μg/mL CDH–CDs and 10 mM singlet oxygen (1O2) scavenger histidine (His), 200 μg/mL CDH–CDs and 10 mM superoxide anion (O2 •-) scavenger p-benzoquinone (PBQ), or sterile water (control) for 45 min, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of the suspensions were observed using an F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), which reflected the generation of ROS in the systems [50].

MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was incubated with terephthalic acid (TA) (1 mM), CDH–CDs (200 μg/mL), or the mixture of TA (1 mM) and CDH–CDs for 1 h, respectively. The generation of •OH in MRSA was monitored by the change in fluorescence intensity of TA.

MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was incubated with 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF, 100 μg/mL) and CDH–CDs at the concentration of 0 (control), 500, or 1000 μg/mL for 45 min. The generation of 1O2 in MRSA was monitored by the change in absorption of DPBF.

MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was treated with cytochrome c (0.5 mg/mL) and CDH–CDs (0, 50.0, 200, or 500 μg/mL) for 45 min. The generation of O2 •- in MRSA was monitored by the change in absorption at 550 nm of cytochrome c.

MRSA (1 × 109 CFU/mL) was incubated with CDH–CDs at the concentration of 0, 50.0, 100, 200, or 400 μg/mL at 37 °C for 4 h and collected by centrifugation. An Ezup Column Bacteria Genomic DNA Purification Kit (B518255, Sangon Biotech) was used to extract DNA from treated bacteria for agarose gel electrophoresis (voltage: 140 V, 45 min). The gel was imaged under a UV lamp (254 nm).

MRSA (1 × 108 CFU/mL) was incubated with 0 (control), or 50.0 μg/mL CDH–CDs at 37 °C for 18 h. The total RNA in treated bacteria was extracted with the TRIzol reagent. Then, cDNA was synthesized by the employment of a RevertAid First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (K1622, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Real-time quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) was conducted to analyze the obtained cDNA using KiCqStart™ SYBR® Green qPCR ReadyMix™ (KCQS0ri0, Sigma-Aldrich). After being searched through the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI), the primer sequence of the target gene was designed using Beacon Designer 8, and the specificity was validated through Blast. The primer sequence was shown in Table S1.

Normal male KM mice (6–8 weeks, Laboratory Animal Center of Hubei Province) were employed as experimental mice to assess in vivo anti-bacterial properties of CDH–CDs. The mice were cared tenderly complying with the guidelines outlined in the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. The procedures were approved by Hubei University of Technology Animal Care and Use Committee with an affidavit of Approval of Animal Ethical and Welfare number HBUT20240014. Briefly, the infected mouse was created by cutting a full-thickness wound (a circle with a diameter of 10 mm) on the dorsal skin. Then, MRSA suspension (10 μL, 1 × 106 CFU/mL) was added to the wound to build the mouse wound infection model. After one day, the infected mice were randomly divided into three groups (5 mice pre-group) and treated with 10 μL PBS (control), 500 μg/mL CDH–CDs or CDH by dropping the samples on the wounds, respectively. The wound area and body weight of each mouse were recorded every 2 days. The area and superposed graphs of the wound surface were analyzed and acquired by ImageJ.

L929 mouse fibroblast cells were incubated with CDH–CDs at different concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.50, 25.00, or 50.00 μg/mL, 100 μL/well) and cultured at 37 °C for 48 h in a 96-well plate. 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, 10 μL, 5 mg/mL) was added to each well and the cells were further incubated for 4 h. After the removal of the solution, 100 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added and the absorption in each well at 490 nm was recorded to calculate the cell viability.

At the end of the in vivo anti-bacterial treatments, the mice were euthanized and the blood samples were collected for blood routine examination (the blood from healthy mice was measured as control). The infected wounds were then collected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and Masson’s trichrome staining. Additionally, the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney from each mouse were harvested and subjected to H&E staining for comparison with those from healthy control mice.

CDH–CDs (12.5, 25.0, 50.0, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL) were incubated with red blood cells at 37 °C for 1 h. The red blood cells in 0.9 % NaCl solution were used as a negative control, while 0.5 wt% Triton-X100 as a positive control. After being centrifuged, each sample was photographed and the supernatant was used to measure the absorbance at 405 nm (OD405). The hemolysis rate at each concentration was calculated using the formula (4).

Quantitative data were presented as means ± standard deviation (S.D.). The sample number (n) expressed the number of each individual experiment sample. Statistical comparisons between groups were performed using Student’s t-test. Significance is indicated by p values (n.s. > 0.05, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

Before that, several characterizations were conducted to investigate the structure features of CDH–CDs. As shown in Fig. S2, CDH–CDs exhibited a negative charge, with a Zeta potential of −9.03 ± 1.58 mV in neutral (pH 7.4) or −6.06 ± 0.62 mV in acidic (pH 5.6) buffer. Compared to precursor CDH, the absorption at 215.3 nm blue shifted to 206.3 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDH–CDs (Fig. S3), which was attributed to the n→π* transition of the -CONH-. Subsequently, we used TEM to examine the morphology of CDH–CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1B and C, CDH–CDs presented as quasi-spherical with a diameter in the range of 1.5–5.0 nm and an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm. The PXRD pattern of CDH–CDs shown in Fig. 1D displayed a broad peak around 25.5°, suggesting the formation of disordered carbon atoms [51]. Furthermore, the retention of surface groups on CDH–CDs was analyzed using an FT-IR spectroscope (Fig. 1E). The FT-IR analysis of both CDH–CDs and its precursor CDH showed peaks corresponding to the stretching vibrations of -NH2 at 3389.50 cm-1, C = O at 1708.89 cm-1, C–N at 1554.22 cm-1, and N–H at 1196.28 cm-1. Additionally, new peaks emerged in the FT-IR spectrum of CDH–CDs at 1323.67 cm⁻¹, 952.61 cm⁻¹, and 704.94 cm⁻¹ compared to that of CDH, which were attributable to the bond stretching of -NO2, C–O, and C–C, respectively [52–54]. These results indicated the formation of new chemical bonds in the synthesis process of CDH–CDs. In addition, the chemical bonding and elementary composition were checked by XPS, which was consistent with the FT-IR results. XPS total curve (Fig. 1F) revealed that CDH–CDs contained carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, and the element proportions were 36.08 %, 43.68 %, and 20.24 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s map (Fig. 1G) exhibited three fitting peaks at 283.58 eV, 284.98 eV, and 287.41 eV, which were pinpointed as the C–C, C–N, and C = O bond, respectively. By inspecting the high-resolution N1s map (Fig. 1H), three peaks measuring 397.47 eV, 399.23 eV, and 400.77 eV were discovered, which represent the -NH2, N–N, and -NO2, respectively. Two peaks at 530.19 eV and 531.44 eV in the high-resolution O1s map (Fig. 1I) were assigned to C–O and C = O bonds. The newly formed bonds contributed to both the structural integrity and anti-bacterial properties of CDH–CDs. For instance, the C–C bonds help stabilize the three-dimensional framework of CDH–CDs, improving the stability and durability of the material [55]. Meanwhile, the -NO2 group, a strong electron-withdrawing moiety with high oxidizing capability[56], conferred the potential anti-bacterial potential of CDH–CDs. Additionally, the C–O bonds on the surface increase the hydrophilicity of CDH–CDs due to oxygen's high electronegativity, enabling hydrogen bonding with water molecules and strengthening the material's interaction with aqueous environments [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further examine the anti-bacterial activity of CDH–CDs in vitro, we first explored the anti-bacterial effect of different doses of CDH–CDs on different bacteria. As shown in Fig. 2 A and B, when incubating with the bacteria at 37 °C for 18 h, CDH–CDs demonstrated a MIC value of 25 μg/mL against ESBL-E or MRSA, which was much lower than that of CDH. In addition, CDH–CDs also bespoke better anti-bacterial capability toward S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. 2C and D), indicating the superior anti-bacterial activity of CDH–CDs. The disk diffusion assays further validated the excellent anti-bacterial effect of CDH–CDs. The diameter of the zone of inhibition (ZOI) of CDH–CDs against MRSA (Fig. 2E and G) or ESBL-E (Fig. 2F and G) was 1.6- to 3.1- or 1.6- to 2.1-fold than that of the precursor CDH, respectively. The monitoring of bacterial growth inhibition proclaimed that the developed CDs at the concentration of 25.0 (MIC) or 50.0 (2 × MIC) μg/mL could effectively inhibit the growth of MRSA or ESBL-E within 24 h. When the concentration of CDH–CDs was lower than MIC (e.g., 12.5 μg/mL, 1/2 MIC), the growth of the bacteria was limited to a certain degree, but ultimately going on (Fig. 3 A and B). However, incubation with CDH precursor at the same concentration gradient still permitted bacterial growth, suggesting its limited anti-bacterial efficacy. Simultaneously, DMAO/PI staining of live/dead bacteria further certificated that the incubation with 800 μg/mL CDH–CDs at 37 °C for 1 h could cause severe damage to both MRSA and ESBL-E to induce death, while most of the bacteria incubated with CDH at the same concentration survived (Fig. 3C and S4). SEM was then used to examine the morphological changes in bacterial surfaces. After the treatment of 800 μg/mL CDH–CDs at 37 °C for 4 h, obvious surface irregularities and collapse of the skeleton of MRSA and ESBL-E were observed (Fig. 3D), confirming that CDH–CDs disrupted bacterial membrane integrity. In contrast, the treatment of CDH preserved the intact surface morphology of the bacteria (Fig. 3D). All these results demonstrated the excellent anti-bacterial activity of CDH–CDs towards various bacteria.

Resistance development is a major concern, which would lead to a decrease in the anti-bacterial efficacy of antibiotics [58,59]. Therefore, we assessed the ability of MRSA and ESBL-E to develop resistance toward CDH–CDs. Two typical antibiotics, rifampicin and kanamycin, were used as control. Serial passaging of the bacteria in the presence of subinhibitory concentrations of the agents was cultured to estimate resistance. As shown in Fig. 3E and F, apparent resistance of MRSA and ESBL-E was developed to rifampicin and kanamycin. Exposure to rifampicin resulted in a 256- or 128-fold increased MIC of MRSA or ESBL-E after 14 passages, respectively. Besides, the MIC for kanamycin had increased 64-fold for both MRSA and ESBL-E after 14 passages. When cultured in the presence of CDH–CDs for 14 passages, no significant change in MIC was observed for MRSA or ESBL-E (MIC of 25 μg/mL at the first passage and 50 μg/mL at the last passage), indicating no resistance to CDH–CDs was developed for both MRSA and ESBL-E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anti-biofilm activity of CDH–CDs
ce:section.ce:para: There followed the assessment of the efficacy against biofilm of CDH–CDs. MRSA or ESBL-E was cultured with CDH or CDH–CDs at 28 °C for 72 h without agitation, and then fixed and stained with crystal violet to quantify the biomass of the as-formed biofilms. As illustrated in Fig. S5, the inhibition rate of biofilm formation was over 90 % when MRSA or ESBL-E was incubated with CDH–CDs at a concentration higher than 100 μg/mL. Nevertheless, a ≥ 90 % inhibition rate of biofilm formation required CDH concentrations reaching 400 μg/mL. Moreover, CDH–CDs were also able to destroy pre-formed biofilms, showing an MBEC of 400 μg/mL for MRSA and 50 μg/mL for ESBL-E, while CDH exhibited an MBEC over 800 μg/mL (Fig. 4 A-D). Biofilm staining also revealed greater bacterial death in MRSA and ESBL-E biofilms treated with CDH–CDs compared to CDH (Fig. 4E and S6). However, there were fewer stained live or dead bacteria when the biofilms were incubated with CDH–CDs, which might be attributed to the removal of most bacteria on the round coverslips. Based on the aforementioned results, we could conclude that CDH–CDs could effectively eradicate both MRSA and ESBL-E biofilms.

Previous reports have summarized the fundamental mechanism responsible for the anti-bacterial activity of CDs, including the generation of massive ROS, disruption of DNA structures, and damage to bacterial membrane structures [60–62]. To explore ROS production in bacteria treated with CDH–CDs, the ROS indicator DCFH-DA was utilized, which was first deacetylated and then oxidized by ROS to yield fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF) intracellularly [63]. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A and S7, the DCF fluorescence intensities of MRSA or ESBL-E treated with 200 μg/mL CDH–CDs significantly increased, indicating the generation of ROS in the bacteria. When selecting IPA, His, and PBQ as scavengers to trap different types of ROS, the DCF fluorescence intensities decreased obviously (Fig. 5A), suggesting that the generated ROS mainly included •OH, 1O2, and O2 •-. Further examination using TA, DPBF, and cytochrome c as indicators substantiated the conclusion that •OH, 1O2, and O2 •- were produced when the bacteria were incubated with CDH–CDs (Fig. 5B-D). Afterwards, we conducted DNA gel electrophoresis to ascertain the disruption of DNA structures. In Fig. S8, it was evident that DNA from bacteria incubating with CDH–CDs at concentrations up to 400 μg/mL kept integrity, suggesting CDH–CDs did not interrupt the DNA structure to kill bacteria. We then investigated the damage to bacterial membrane structures. The morphological changes in bacterial surfaces after the treatment of CDH–CDs have been proved using SEM in Fig. 3D The expression of genes connected to MRSA membrane synthesis and bacterial division was subsequently explored through qPCR. After incubation with CDH–CDs, the expression of FabI, FemA, FtsA, FtsL, FtsZ, MraY, MurB, and MurC in MRSA was increased (Fig. 5E). When bacteria were treated with a drug, these genes might be up-regulated to maintain the stability of the cell walls, repair damaged cell walls, or accelerate bacterial division, to fight against the threat. Meanwhile, the down-regulation of MurA was also observed (Fig. 5E), which inhibited the synthesis of peptidoglycan, an important component of the cell walls, through down-regulating the expression of UDP-GlcNAc transferase [64,65]. Above all, we hypothesized that CDH–CDs could damage and inhibit the repair of the bacterial cell walls, then promoting the generation of endogenous ROS including •OH, 1O2, and O2 •- to kill bacteria (Fig. 5F) [66]. These aspects resulted in the marvelous anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo anti-bacterial activity of CDH–CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To sum up, we have developed an effective anti-bacterial agent CDH–CDs through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. CDH–CDs manifested superior anti-bacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria S. aureus, Gram-negative bacteria E. coli, and two kinds of drug-resistant bacteria MRSA and ESBL-E. The MIC of CDH–CDs toward these bacteria was 25.0 or 50.0 μg/mL, which was much smaller than that of the precursor CDH. In contrast to conventional antibiotics such as rifampicin and kanamycin, MRSA and ESBL-E exhibited significantly reduced capacity to develop resistance toward CDH–CDs. Besides, CDH–CDs also exhibited superior anti-biofilm efficacy with an MBEC of 400 μg/mL for MRSA and 50 μg/mL for ESBL-E. Notably, CDH–CDs efficiently killed the bacteria through damaging bacterial cell walls and affecting gene expression, then promoting the generation of endogenous ROS to disrupt bacterial membrane structures and affecting gene expression to eliminate bacteria. In vivo anti-bacterial studies further evidenced the good anti-bacterial performance of CDH–CDs, providing a facile method to overcome bacterial infection.
